speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Martin Selling. The date is April 1, 1996. We left off talking about Kristallnacht, being arrested, being sent to prison in Ansbach. Half of the people were released. You had to remain in prison, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And one thing I want to say about Kristallnacht, I had an uncle in Lehrberg who also was arrested. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over 55. I don't know exactly how old. And he was in prison, and questioned for some reason or another, for three days. He came out, was released by the Gestapo, went home and committed suicide. He hung himself. He was a bachelor. Before that, that uncle I mentioned, who volunteered for military service through World War I-- twice-- and was wounded. And he was decorated. It was his Germany. He was a German all the way over. And when, in 1937, he finally was brought to realize that he wasn't a German, and it wasn't his Germany at all, he just couldn't take it. And he committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we talking about two different people now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one was a brother of my father's, the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siegfried Selling. And the other one was a brother of my mother. His name was Huckel Laub.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they both committed suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were quite a few suicides at that time. People who couldn't face it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in the prison? You said half of the people were released. You remained with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were put on a truck and shipped to Lehrberg, as I said. There that prison was full and crowded. So they didn't even have room for us anymore. So they put us in the gym, threw in some mattresses, some honey buckets-- not enough-- and just kept us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many men would you say where there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Totally, about 100 to begin with. Most of them were from Lehrberg, Furth, and the facilities there. Because evidently, Dachau was  overcrowded and they couldn't take too many at a time. One of reasons the prison was overcrowded is they had about 400 Sudetendeutsche or Czechs from the Sudetenland-- Germans who did not like the idea that Hitler moved into Czechoslovakia, and they were imprisoned. At that time the prisons were full of Germans who were liberated by their friends from their homeland. And that was quite a phenomenon, which was unexpected here. Those people expected Hitler to march in and liberate them from Czech oppression. But the people who marched in was the SS, who had a list of everybody who said something nasty about them and arrested them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just Jews that were in the gym at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were just Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated in the gym?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were left alone as long as-- the guards were old police people. They were quite sympathetic. They did their duty, and nothing more. And so finally, when we dwindled, and as they started releasing people, after we were about 20 or so, 30, we were put into cells. And then they started to release it. First, I was in the cell with somebody else. Then I was alone. And when we were dwindled down to nine, eight were shipped to Dachau. And one was released that day. That was the 22nd of December, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they released?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole action was not meant to be permanent. It just was meant, from what I can see, it was as a shock treatment and a warning to everybody else. We can get nasty, as if they needed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they did it slowly and with as much difficulty as possible. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so when we were eight, we were shipped to Dachau. And in the same train, the same railroad car even, there were 90 people from Czechoslovakia, Sudetenland, who were shipped there, too. So how did they decide who was going to be released and who was going to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Largely age, to personal feelings of the local Gestapo, and other considerations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interrogated at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, for what?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, they just chose--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just wanted to show for some retribution. As you are aware of, it was supposed to be in retribution of a murder committed in Paris by a Jew against a Nazi official a week earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know more about that story? Do you know who the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vom Rath.

speaker: interviewer
text: The murder was committed by--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were by a-- said at that time. And to answer your question, it was a  Jewish fellow named Grynszpan. And he murdered the counselor of the German legacy in Paris, vom Rath. The rumors were that it was-- [INAUDIBLE] said vom Rath was-- meant it for-- was meant for elimination. And they set it up, used it as a way to find an excuse for Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your uncle still with you when you were with the eight?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was released earlier, on December the 4th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any other men you knew that were held?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew from prison-- that-- and one of them, we became very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Ernst Thingfelder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thingfelder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thingfelder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he came from Furth. He was a bachelor at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ernst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ernst. So you were taken on a train, correct? Did they tell you where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we knew where we were going, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about four hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train it was all right. It started getting bad after we got off the train. Actually, we were in a-- a long distance train car. It was a better car, better railroad car. But as we got to Dachau, where we got out of the train, things changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the first thing you saw or when you got out  of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw a bunch of SS guards with bayonets and loaded rifles. They asked us to get out and chased us on a coal car. There was a local train, which was used to bring coal to Dachau. And they made us jump up there and helped us, so-called, with their bayonets a little bit to get up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we eight Jews from Lehrberg were separated. And it was almost impossible to get on top of that car. So, I being the youngest and almost the strongest I would say, helped the others up. And I had a hard time stocking up myself. So we were pushed into the camp, we're told to jump off. They had a big shower there in the processing room. We were-- at one end we were stripped all the way down, got a medically-- so-called medically, whatever it is-- got a shower. Actually it was the other way around. And got prison clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody grabbed the biggest shoes they could. So when it was my turn, I had a big foot, there was no shoe for me. So another older prisoner, who was a trustee, brought me a pair of sandals made out of rubber for a tire, from old car tires. And that's what I wore for about a week or two, till I finally got boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me make sure I understand the story correctly. When you arrived-- first of all, did you know what Dachau was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about it. I actually heard about it quite early.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear? What did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, in April, a second cousin of mine, who was a lawyer in Munich, was arrested and sent to Dachau. And he died just three months later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got there, you were-- this is the part I want to make sure I understand-- you were told to go on top of a coal car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to get us into camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to get into the coal car. And then you had to jump out of the coal car?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an open one, one of those open [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] railroad cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to jump on top of the coal car and over it to the other side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was an open car, a flatbed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted to just go up on top of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place to sit. It was an open car.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they first made you go up there, and then you had to jump off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then they pulled us with a rickety engine into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got into the camp from this coal car?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the railroad station to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were told to get off the coal car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when we were in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were then sent directly to shower, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were into the processing room, where we were stripped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the commandants, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but another interesting thing in that process. I mentioned I had a friend there. He was shipped with me to Dachau. He was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ernst? Is this Ernst that you --

speaker: interviewee
text: Ernst Thingfelder. And he wanted to bring his tefellin into the camp. We dissuaded him. They probably would have killed him if he would have tried to take them along. So that really was a scene do get him to leave his tallit and tefellin behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he leave it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There with all the rest of our clothes. They were just pushed in a bag. And when we got out we got that bag back. There was-- and all we had, what we could take along-- we were stark naked-- was whatever we could put it our handkerchief and carry alongside, like a little bit of money and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who examined you? You said you underwent a medical examination?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-called, they saw whether we still breathed, were breathing. And we did, evidently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were showered? Were you given any number at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number? Can you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: 31889. That means I was the 31,000th person. Whoever went to Dachau, they went by numbers. The first one who came to Dachau from the Kristallnacht, the numbering started in 18800. And so there were about 12,000 Jews from the Kristallnacht in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the number? Was it put on you anyplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the left breast.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right breast. On the right breast, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the clothing, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the uniform look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a prison striped blue and white. It was winter and they didn't have enough prison uniforms. In Dachau they had summer uniforms and winter uniforms. So we got the summer uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in December.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the shower and giving the number, then what happened to you? Where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were pushed to a barracks, which they had. And we had in double time. Everything was moving in double time. And the guards rode along on bicycles. We were not allowed to walk-- we had to run all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So about midnight, we got into that little room in the barracks, which was crowded wall to wall with prisoners. How many people would you say were inside the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 200 in this particular room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the barracks? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called a block. It consisted of four, let's say, apartments with a living room and a bedroom, so to speak. It was supposed to hold 50 people, 50 beds. But before we came, long before, it was already in July from what I understand, they started to prepare for that. And they put wooden boards along the walls and the shelves. And we had some straw. And that just was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the barracks have a number? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What number was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was block eight, room four.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the physical layout of Dachau? What did it look like? Can you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I could do justice of this-- I mean, I may not even attempt to do it. That has been documented someplace. And everybody can find out what it actually was. So better than I ever could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your recollections of the way it looked though?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dreary, cold, wet, and just like a prison [INAUDIBLE] look.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surrounded by barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Two rows.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the sanitary conditions like inside the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the barracks they were good. It had a wash room, which had to be kept clean. And they had toilets, water-- toilets-- which had to be kept clean. Actually, they were so clean that a lot of punishment resulted from not being clean enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of punishments did you observe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be beaten up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For nothing. I don't know. Because I was in the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards, SS guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was actually in charge of your-- was there somebody in charge of your particular block?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- they had trustees, prisoners who are in charge of the block, and one of each room. And they couldn't do much anyway. They were said to-- they had to make sure that we kept the place clean, that we did everything we should do. Otherwise they would get punished. And being a trustee, by its nature, was a little cushier than the rest of the prison life. So they would not risk losing that job if we fall down. So they made sure we toed the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were other fellow prisoners that were called trustees, as you're calling them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall anybody specifically who was the trustee? Can you describe that person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But let me wait to the end when I make my comments. And I would like to describe that situation by itself. For it will lead to my conclusions.

speaker: interviewer
text: The- Everybody that was in your barracks, were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of jobs were you assigned to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any job, regular jobs, which is hard labor. We were not put in hard labor because they just didn't have enough facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of things did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- everybody had to be out of the barracks in the morning after breakfast. So we either stood around freezing at the parade grounds, or we ran around to get warm-- of course in groups, not individually. And it was-- we was-- we were exercised.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of exercise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running around. And quite a few collapsed from the rigor of the weather and the conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to stand at roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. Roll call was twice a day, in the morning at 5 o' clock, and in the evening at 6:00. And during one of the roll calls, towards the end of my stay, one prisoner had escaped. And the commandant said, you stay here and-- and stand here till that prisoner's been recaptured. So we were standing there all night at attention. And some of us fell down and couldn't stay at attention. Since there were several thousand people, those more or less in the middle had it a little easier. I must have dozed off, and I suddenly I was still on my feet. I was hit in the back with a rifle butt. Luckily for me, I did not hit the ground. I could catch myself and got up. Or they would have killed me right then and there. That morning, at 6 o'clock after sunrise, we were allowed one hour to go back and have breakfast. The place was littered with people who had collapsed-- quite a few. That is they were all dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the person that escaped, was he found?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was recaptured the next evening. And I remember when he was brought back to the camp. And then we finally were allowed to go back to our barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure he died-- not easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Swill. Swill, what you feed the pigs. We had every three days, two shared a loaf of bread. And three times a day we had to go to the kitchen, got big buckets of coffee and mostly tapioca. And in the evening it was stew, as I described already before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, talking about what we were fed. That friend of mine, Ernst Thingfelder, he was strictly orthodox kosher. He recoiled of eating that stews, which was everything together. So I gave him most of my bread, and I ate the meat-- for he wouldn't touch the meat. So I practically didn't eat any bread there. And we both got out. Were you in the barracks with him, the same barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the same barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people slept on the floorboards you said they put in? How many were you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started out with 200. Originally the barracks held 300. But as I said-- I'm talking about our room alone. If you want to talk about the barracks, you multiply it by four. We were pushed in, as I said. And later as more and more were released, it was just at the day when I was released, they started to reconvert the barracks to the original bunks, which held 52 people per room. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before that, we actually dwindled down from 200 to 100 and more. We were lucky, we had a few more blankets. We had a little more room. We didn't have to sleep practically on top of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the nature of any conversation you might have had with Ernst at night time, or any of the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk. Lights out and you shut up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: The big question was, why? And I compared it, being a history buff-- even at that young age, had read a lot of history, which included the crusades, the inquisition, all that stuff. I said, what is the difference of it? What's the difference between-- and to put it very frankly, between suffering ad majorem Dei glorium, which is the slogan of the Jesuits, or just for the hell of it? I couldn't find any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you state that again? I'm sorry, I  had trouble understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ad majorem Dei glorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say what that means?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means for the greater glory of God. That is the slogan of the Jesuits. And just for the hell of it is what we went through. And I couldn't see much difference. And that is all the same God. Lots of people came out of Dachau very religious. I think more of them-- and I was one of them-- without religion. That's where I really stopped believing, if I ever believed much. But I continued to go through the motions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Ernst able to-- your friend or any other religious people in Dachau at the time-- were they able to conduct any religious observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no way. Nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you do not remember the name of the commandant? Or do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened in Dachau? Anything else that you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's enough. But we were talking about food. There was within the prison of Dachau, there was a compound with the barbed wire, with three barracks separated, which was reserved-- or it wasn't reserved-- which was the compound for the Jehovah's Witnesses. They were there because they refused to swear allegiance to the flag, or to the country or whatever else it is. And they were kept there till they got up and swore allegiance. They just didn't. They were the only cohesive groups there who was a little bit-- who was concerned about their fellow prisoners. And one of them was caught sharing his bread with somebody outside that particular compound. So the commandant ruled, evidently we feed you too much. Instead of half a loaf, you get only a third of a loaf. That was three men to a loaf of bread. And since they believed in the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the sabbath, or Sunday, as a day of rest, they were made to work seven days a week. Usually labor in Dachau was six days a week. But they had-- they was all seven days.
